title: Hippocrepis squamata
intro: Hippocrepis squamata, also known as the hairy needle-plant or hairy rosin-root, is a perennial plant species within the pea family (Fabaceae). The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include its anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial activities, contributing to its potential benefits in treating various health conditions, such as wound healing, respiratory issues, and digestive problems. The bioactive constituents of Hippocrepis squamata include flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which are responsible for its therapeutic effects. Traditional herbal preparations made from this plant include infusions, decoctions, and tinctures, often used to treat fever, cough, and sore throat, as well as to promote skin and wound healing.
taxonomy: Hippocrepis squamata is classified within the family Fabaceae. This classification places it among the legume family, which comprises plants that have fruits in the form of pods and seeds in the form of beans or peas. The genus Hippocrepis is a group of flowering plants commonly known as hairy vetchlings or horsehoof. The plant is further classified as a member of the subfamily Faboideae, which contains many other legume species. This classification system reflects the plant's evolutionary relationships with other plants in the Fabaceae family.
